utterances: You know, and there - they were saying votes to be counted.
It's worth pointing out in all these states that - that Trump blew up that - that blue Democratic wall, like Domenico was saying, it's about a point difference or less.
So I think they felt like they could - they could make it happen with cities coming in.
And then it became clear that the votes just weren't there.
Domenico, let me finish with a question a lot of minds this morning.
How in the world did pollsters get this so wrong?
You know, I have a theory on this, and we'll see what the - what actually happens.
But, you know, you have seen - I've seen over a decade, at least, where, you know, the mainstream media has been - really had the carpet taken out from under it, especially with Republicans.
And there's a huge level of distrust.
Think about the voters who Donald Trump has - has turned out - you know, blue collar voters who had severe distrust of the media, distrust of institutions.

There's the possibility that when all these pollsters called and they identified themselves as being from that big media organization that they didn't pick up the phone or they didn't want to talk to them or they kept that - hung up that phone.

Now, that's usually what happens in our - after big breaking news events, why you often don't want to poll, you know, that - within a week or so of that big news event because it's artificially inflated.
That is possibly - possibly one theory of what went wrong.
OK, that's NPR's lead political editor, Domenico Montanaro, and NPR campaign reporter Scott Detrow.
Thanks, both of you.
Sure thing.
Thank you.
A Missouri soldier goes to Iraq, comes home, and finds that everything he owns is gone.
Fortunately, as Missy Shelton of member station KSMU reports, the story is headed toward a happy ending.
Patrick Rogalin lives in an apartment in Springfield, Missouri.
But most of what's in his apartment belongs to his girlfriend.
This table, definitely not mine.
If you go into the kitchen, that's her coffee pot.
All the silverware and dishes and stuff is hers.
All the silverware and dishes I have were sold.
While Rogalin was on active duty overseas, something went awry with his automatic payments to Public Storage, the company storing his belongings.
When payments were late, Public Storage auctioned off Patrick Rogalin's things.
He says that shouldn't have happened, since he told the company he would be serving in Iraq, and even called from the Middle East to tell them his payments would be late.
I called them and made them know that there would be a problem.
You know, and they weren't happy about it but they said they were going to make a note of it, just like they said they were going to make a note of it when I left.
But they never did.
Clem Teng is the vice president of investor relations for Public Storage.
He says the company didn't know Rogalin was on active duty.
This incident is inconsistent with the company's policies and procedures with regard to known active duty military personnel.
And really, it resulted from an inability to communicate with the customer at the time that he rented the unit.
Soon after news of Rogalin's situation showed up on Internet postings, hundreds of people tried to contact him with offers of money and furniture.
One woman paid for the books he'll need at the local university when classes start next week.
Others like David Holtz(ph), a physician in Fort Lauderdale, Florida, say they still want to help.
That's sad, you know, to treat a returning veteran from this another horrible war we're involved in over there.
But these boys, they go over there and put their life on the line, they deserve a lot of consideration and respect, and any assistance we can give them.
It's been hard for Holtz and others to reach Rogalin because the attention caught him off guard, and he's been reluctant to give out his girlfriend's address.
Instead, he's encouraging people to donate to charities for the homeless.
Rogalin now has a $4,000 check from Public Storage, though he claims his possessions were worth more than double that.
And money will never replace some of the things.
Photos of family, photos of me as a baby.
They're material items, but what you have in your home makes it your home.
I expected to try to make the apartment I live now, you know, my home.
And it was really hard to not have those items that, you know, I had for so long to go back to.

Patrick Rogalin says he hopes that by sharing his story, Public Storage and similar businesses will learn how to work better with soldiers serving overseas, so that when they return home they don't face the same fate.
For NPR News, I'm Missy Shelton in Springfield, Missouri.
No, ma'am.
No, ma'am.
He's a decent family man, citizen, that I just happen to have disagreements with.
Because she was right.
It's simple as that.
And it's very interesting.
We are redoing Moses projects all over the country, including taking down expressways and highways that tore through the genuine fabric of the cities.
The point about Jane's durability is that Jane respected the real residents and business owners in a city.
She understood because she observed what worked - that you have to listen to the local voices to understand what would help rejuvenate or build on a local place without replacing it.
Roberta Brandes Gratz is an urban critic.
She's one of the founders of The Center for the Living City, which advances the work of Jane Jacobs.
Roberta Brandes Gratz, thank you so much for speaking with us.
My pleasure, Michel.
Virtual reality is pitched as an experience that will revolutionize movies and video games.
And there are scientists who think it can revolutionize the way we eat, too.
Turnstile News reporter Noah Nelson became a guinea pig for a virtual reality eating lab in Los Angeles.
I've got a problem - a fraught relationship with food, none more so than doughnuts.
This is Sidecar Doughnuts, one of the best doughnut places on Earth.
In the interest of journalism, I recently visited their newly opened Santa Monica branch a few miles, thankfully, from NPR West.
Let me get a Pumpkin Fool because seasonal, of course, and a Butter and Salt, as well.
Are the Cinnamon Crumbs seasonal or are they - they're regular?
OK, then I can avoid them for the moment.
Not that I want to avoid them, and that's the problem.
Why is it that the good things are always bad for us?
This is designer Jinsoo An, and he might just have a solution to my problem.
Maybe with virtual reality that doesn't need to be the case.
An's virtual reality eating experience aims to let people eat whatever they want without the downside, be that because of unwanted calories, food allergies or another medical condition.
He calls it Project Nourished.
To find out what it's all about, I visited his studio in downtown Los Angeles.
So we're in the kitchen, and we were actually making some sushi last night.
He shows me a couple of semi-translucent cubes that have been molded to look like rice.
They're made out of fish-flavored agar-agar, a vegan substitute for gelatin.
You're actually one of the first ones to try this.
You have no idea how giddy that makes me.
I pull the Oculus Rift goggles over my head and dig in, virtually.
Hello.
Momentarily, I will guide you through a culinary experience of a lifetime.
Inside the goggles, I see a little table overlooking a Zen garden, and there's a plate with a tiny cube of sushi rice.
And then I actually smell sushi.
(SOUNDBITE OF ATOMIZER)
That really loud sound right there is an atomizer, usually used to mist medicine, but repurposed here to create that archetypal sushi restaurant smell.
Using a pair of motion-tracking chopsticks, I pick up the fake rice cube in the VR and real world and miss my mouth a couple of times before taking a bite.
The smell and the flavor - like, I'm definitely tasting fish - like, totally tasting fish.
There's just a hint of fish broth, but the sense was of being whacked in the mouth with fish.
That illusion was put together with the help of restaurateur Nguyen Tran.
We found that the, like, defining flavors of sushi, at least for the American palate, is ginger and wasabi.
And the minute we put those in there and it layered it on top of just a simple later of dashi, rice and seaweed, it was exactly like sushi for us.
Right now, Project Nourished requires a touch of suspension of disbelief.
But designer An sees it as an evolving open canvas for experimentation.
Which means that we can insert nutrients and take away nutrients.
You can change the behavior of the food however you want.
That's what's so magical about this.
It turns food into a piece of code.
So maybe one day we could pack all the nutrition I need into a virtual, guilt-free Pumpkin Fool doughnut.
For NPR News, I'm Noah Nelson.
That story was produced by Turnstyle News, a project of Youth Radio.
When an audience member at a Donald Trump event asserted that President Obama is a Muslim not born in the United States, Trump did not challenge it.
And now a backlash after that exchange has put Trump on the defensive.
NPR national political correspondent Don Gonyea reports this was just one part of a tough week for the presidential candidate.
At his town hall in New Hampshire last night, Trump made an opening statement then went right to the audience for Q and A.
